fast.summary: This paper proposes Multi-layer Representations (MLR), which uses vectors from multiple encoder layers (not just the last) to represent documents in dense retrieval. They show this improves retrieval quality and can be pooled down to single-vector representations for efficiency.
fast.key_idea: Different transformer layers encode different linguistic knowledge, so combining representations across layers produces richer document embeddings than using only the final layer.
fast.why_it_matters: Practitioners can improve retrieval accuracy in RAG pipelines and search systems with minimal architectural changes, using the same pre-trained encoders they already have.
possible_use_cases: Improving document retrieval quality in RAG-based LLM applications
Enhancing semantic search engines for technical or scientific corpora
Boosting passage retrieval in open-domain question answering systems

deep.method_breakdown: MLR uses representations from multiple hidden layers of a BERT-based document encoder rather than only the final layer, concatenating or combining these layer-wise representations to form richer document embeddings. Under the multi-vector setting, each layer contributes its own vector, and pooling strategies (e.g., mean pooling across layers) are applied to collapse multi-vector representations back to single vectors for efficiency. The resulting single-vector representation is then used in standard ANN retrieval pipelines.
deep.key_innovation: Prior dense retrieval work (DPR, ME-BERT, ColBERT) exclusively uses the last hidden layer representation, ignoring the linguistically diverse information encoded at intermediate layers. MLR is the first systematic study showing that aggregating multi-layer representations — and then distilling them back to single vectors via pooling — consistently outperforms last-layer-only baselines without sacrificing retrieval efficiency.
deep.technical_insight: The critical design choice is the pooling strategy that reduces multi-vector (multi-layer) representations to a single vector, preserving the expressiveness of multi-layer semantics while maintaining the O(1) query-time complexity of single-vector retrieval. This works because lower layers capture syntactic and lexical signals while upper layers capture semantic abstractions; pooling fuses complementary information that no single layer alone can provide.
deep.limitations: The approach increases encoder memory and compute during indexing since all layer representations must be computed and pooled, which can be costly for large corpora. The optimal pooling strategy and layer selection may be domain- or task-specific, requiring additional tuning. The paper does not deeply investigate asymmetric encoder designs (query vs. document using different layers), and gains may diminish when the pre-trained model is very large or already heavily fine-tuned.
interview_questions: Why do different layers of a pre-trained language model like BERT encode different kinds of information, and how does MLR exploit this?
How does MLR maintain single-vector retrieval efficiency despite using representations from multiple layers?
What pooling strategies were investigated, and which performed best for reducing multi-vector to single-vector representations?
How does MLR compare to ColBERT, which also uses multi-vector representations but in a fundamentally different way?
Can MLR be combined with hard negative mining and retrieval-oriented pre-training, and does combining them yield additive gains?
interview_answers: BERT layers progressively transform token embeddings from surface-level syntactic features (early layers) to abstract semantic representations (later layers). MLR exploits this by treating each layer's representation as a complementary view of the document, so the combined representation captures a broader linguistic spectrum than the final layer alone.

MLR applies pooling (e.g., mean pooling) across the layer-wise vectors to produce a single fixed-dimensional embedding, which is then stored in the ANN index. At query time, the query encoder also produces a single vector, so retrieval reduces to a standard dot-product search with no additional latency compared to DPR.

The paper investigates mean pooling and learned/weighted pooling over selected layers. Mean pooling over all or a subset of layers generally performs well, with the insight that intermediate layers (not just the last) contribute meaningfully; overly including very early layers can hurt performance due to their less task-relevant representations.

ColBERT stores a vector per token of a document and performs late interaction via MaxSim at query time, making it expressive but storage- and compute-intensive. MLR stores vectors per layer and pools them into one, achieving comparable or better expressiveness in a single-vector format that is far more efficient in both storage and query latency.

Yes, MLR is orthogonal to training strategies. The paper shows that combining MLR with hard negative mining and retrieval-oriented pre-training (e.g., ICT, BFS objectives) yields additive improvements, suggesting that better training data and better representation architecture address complementary aspects of the retrieval problem.
